# Organic-First Campaign Plan — Small Legal-Tech Tool for Family Lawyers

---

## 0. Assumptions

Mode C — terse brief. Every field below was defaulted because the brief did not specify it. Confirm or adjust any row and I will re-run the affected stages.

| Field | Assumed Value | Source |
|---|---|---|
| Outcome | Trial signups / paid subscriptions for the legal-tech tool | Most plausible conversion goal for a SaaS legal-tech tool; not stated in brief |
| Audience | Family lawyers in private practice (solo / small-firm), English-speaking market, likely US/UK/AU | Inferred from "family lawyers"; geography not specified |
| Sector | b2b-saas | Legal-tech SaaS sold to professional practitioners |
| User budget | <$1k/month | Not stated; assumed floor for a small product outspent 100x |
| Competitor spend | 100x user budget (stated) — classified as categorical asymmetry (1:100+) | Stated in brief |
| Existing channels / traction | Starting from zero — no newsletter, community, or follower base indicated | Not stated |
| Bottleneck | Trust — small tool vs. institutional incumbent; lawyers are risk-averse buyers | 2025–2026 default for underdog b2b-saas; trust is the conversion blocker |
| Time window | 60-day sprint to first 50 paying users, then recurring cadence | Default under Mode C |
| Capacity | 6 hours/week (founder solo or very small team) | Default under Mode C |
| Competition specifics | LexisNexis named in brief; other major legal-research incumbents not named and will not be named | Industry-peer rule applied — escape-hatch phrasing used throughout |

---

## 1. Campaign Ideas (5+ concepts across archetypes)

I am describing the competitive landscape at the category level: LexisNexis is the named incumbent; additional dominant legal-research platforms exist and are part of the saturation picture, but they are not named — I will refer to them as "the other major legal-research platforms" or "LexisNexis-class tools."

---

### Concept A — "The $400 Seat Tax"

**One-line thesis:** Name the monthly cost a solo family lawyer pays for LexisNexis seat access relative to their actual usage, and position your tool as the first one built for volume-light practitioners who pay enterprise rates for enterprise infrastructure they don't need.

**Archetype:** Counter-narrative / pricing-truth

**Primary channel tier:** Tier 1 (founder content — LinkedIn + legal forums) + Tier 2 (SEO long-tail)

**Authenticity hook:** Pull one real invoice line item — or cite publicly available LexisNexis pricing tiers — and show exactly what a solo family lawyer pays per research session vs. what the same session costs on your tool. Numbers are the message.

**Minimum viable version:** One LinkedIn post by the founder with a real cost breakdown table. No video, no design budget. Publish Tuesday morning, track saves and shares for 48 hours before any boost decision.

---

### Concept B — "The Family Law Practitioner's Notebook"

**One-line thesis:** Become the most useful free resource for family lawyers — a weekly digest of family-law developments, case summaries, and practice tips — so that when lawyers discover the tool, they already trust the brand.

**Archetype:** Community-build + newsletter flywheel

**Primary channel tier:** Tier 1 (newsletter) + Tier 2 (SEO / organic search)

**Authenticity hook:** The founder (or an embedded family-law attorney) curates the digest with genuine practitioner judgment — not automated aggregation. The editorial voice is the signal; LexisNexis cannot replicate editorial intimacy at scale.

**Minimum viable version:** One issue of the newsletter sent to 20 personal contacts who are family lawyers or adjacent. Measure open rate and reply rate before committing to a recurring cadence.

---

### Concept C — "Founder Switched" (Defector Story Arc)

**One-line thesis:** If the founder or an early customer has a personal story of cancelling their LexisNexis subscription (or the other major legal-research platforms) and building / switching to the tool, that story is the campaign — the authentic defection narrative that institutional tools cannot manufacture.

**Archetype:** Founder-story arc / defector narrative

**Primary channel tier:** Tier 1 (LinkedIn long-form post + short video) + Tier 3 (earned media — legal-tech press)

**Authenticity hook:** The lived moment of cancelling the subscription — the exact dollar figure saved, the specific workflow that changed, the first client matter handled without the incumbents. Specific, verifiable, irreproducible.

**Minimum viable version:** A single 800-word LinkedIn post from the founder narrating the switch. No agency production. Publish and observe organic reach for 48 hours.

---

### Concept D — "Solo Family Lawyer Challenge"

**One-line thesis:** Invite 10 solo family lawyers to use your tool free for 30 days in exchange for honest public feedback — turning early users into campaign co-creators and proof points simultaneously.

**Archetype:** Referral flywheel / community proof engine

**Primary channel tier:** Tier 1 (community DMs, LinkedIn) + Tier 2 (UGC/testimonials)

**Authenticity hook:** The lawyers' own words, cases, and practice contexts — not marketing copy. Their public reviews are the campaign material. You cannot fake 10 independent practitioners vouching for your tool in their own professional language.

**Minimum viable version:** DM 15 family lawyers identified via LinkedIn or legal bar association directories. Offer 30-day free access in exchange for one public LinkedIn comment after 30 days. Measure: how many say yes, how many post.

---

### Concept E — "Search Where LexisNexis Isn't" (Demand Capture)

**One-line thesis:** Family lawyers searching for "affordable family law research tool," "LexisNexis alternative for small firms," or "family law case research under [price point]" are already trying to find you. Own those queries before LexisNexis's SEO team notices the long tail.

**Archetype:** Demand capture / SEO flywheel

**Primary channel tier:** Tier 2 (SEO) + Tier 1 (content supporting keyword clusters)

**Authenticity hook:** Practitioner-written content that answers the exact questions family lawyers type at 11pm before a hearing — not generic legal research guides. Specificity to the family-law niche (custody case research, QDRO procedures, jurisdiction-specific family code) is the moat.

**Minimum viable version:** Publish three long-form articles targeting the highest-volume low-competition queries in the family-law research niche. Use Google Search Console after 30 days to identify which queries are gaining impressions.

---

### Concept F — "The 60-Minute Research Guarantee" (Earned-Media Stunt)

**One-line thesis:** Make a falsifiable public claim — your tool completes a defined family-law research task in under 60 minutes; LexisNexis's standard workflow takes longer — and invite legal-tech journalists and bar association publications to run the test live.

**Archetype:** Earned-media stunt / proof-of-capability

**Primary channel tier:** Tier 1 (earned media pitch) + Tier 2 (video documentation)

**Authenticity hook:** The timed test must be real. A journalist or bar association member runs it, not you. The result stands regardless of outcome — a close result is still credible coverage for a David vs. Goliath narrative.

**Minimum viable version:** Run the test yourself first, document it on video, post to LinkedIn. If results are credible, pitch one legal-tech journalist with the video as supporting evidence.

---

**Selection:** Because this is Mode C and I cannot ask the user interactively, I am proceeding with **Concept E (Demand Capture / SEO flywheel) as the primary recommended shape** combined with **Concept A (Counter-narrative pricing truth) as the organic content engine**, because:

1. Categorical asymmetry (100x) means cold paid is off the table entirely.
2. Family lawyers have high-intent search behavior — they search for tools before buying.
3. The pricing counter-narrative generates Tier 1 organic content that also supports SEO.

The user should review all six concepts and redirect if a different one better matches their situation.

---

## 2. Selected Concept

**Primary:** Concept E — Demand Capture (SEO flywheel targeting family-law-specific long-tail queries where LexisNexis-class tools are overkill or overpriced)

**Supporting content engine:** Concept A — "The $400 Seat Tax" counter-narrative (LinkedIn + founder content) feeds trust and supports SEO with branded search volume over time.

**Rationale:** At 100x asymmetry, organic trust compounding is the only viable path. The demand-capture approach intercepts lawyers who are already searching — it does not require manufacturing demand from scratch. The counter-narrative pricing content gives the brand a point of view that differentiates without requiring a large audience to begin.

---

## 3. Spend Asymmetry Verdict

**Categorical asymmetry (1:100+).** At this ratio, every dollar of paid media you spend is statistical noise against LexisNexis's signal; cold-paid acquisition will not move the needle, and attempting it will burn runway that the organic-first playbook needs.

**What this means for your strategy:** Paid channels are off the table for awareness and acquisition. You win by being the tool that appears when a family lawyer searches a specific question, by being the founder whose LinkedIn post a family lawyer saves and comes back to, and by being the word-of-mouth recommendation inside bar association Slack groups and Facebook communities — channels where LexisNexis's institutional voice is structurally disadvantaged.

### Preconditions Check (score 0–6)

Scoring against the six organic-first preconditions:

1. **Credible insider / founder story:** Assumed present (you built the tool, presumably from practice or close proximity to family law). If the founder is not a practicing or former family lawyer, this precondition is weaker — flag it. **Score: 1 (assumed; verify)**
2. **Accumulated grievance or unmet demand:** Present and strong. Solo family lawyers are systematically overcharged by enterprise legal-research pricing; the grievance is real, documented, and widely felt. **Score: 1**
3. **Consolidated challenger field:** Unknown — are there other small legal-tech tools targeting family lawyers? If the field is fragmented with 5 "LexisNexis alternatives," your organic narrative dilutes. If you are the clearest alternative in the family-law niche, the precondition is met. **Score: 0.5 (assumed; verify)**
4. **Felt pain, not abstract pain:** Strong. The cost of a LexisNexis seat is a line item on every small-firm P&L. Time wasted on an over-engineered tool is felt on every research session. The pain is concrete and recurring. **Score: 1**
5. **Threshold-rewarding market:** Moderate. Legal-tech has network effects (referrals within bar associations, practice groups, law school alumni networks) but they are slower than consumer network effects. If you reach critical mass in a specific jurisdiction or practice-area community, the compounding accelerates. **Score: 0.5**
6. **Incumbent overplaying saturation:** Yes. LexisNexis competes on comprehensiveness, authority, and institutional presence — exactly the register that feels mismatched to a solo family lawyer doing custody case research. Their production value and enterprise messaging is the gap you exploit. **Score: 1**

**Total: 5/6 preconditions present (or assumed present with flags on #1 and #3).**

**Verdict: Run the organic-first playbook at full scale.** Flag precondition #1 (founder credibility) and #3 (consolidated challenger field) as campaign sub-goals to verify in week 1. If the founder is not from practice, identify and recruit a credible practicing family lawyer as a content collaborator or advisory voice in the first 30 days.

---

## 3a. Message-Market-Fit Gate

Scoring the three MMF signals against the brief:

1. **Revenue/commitment signal:** Unknown — brief does not indicate paying users. Default assumption: tool is early-stage, fewer than 10 paying users. **Signal: No (assumed)**
2. **Language signal:** Unknown — brief does not name a specific pain the audience articulates. The generic "outspent 100x" framing suggests the user knows the competitive problem but may not have extracted the audience's language yet. **Signal: No (assumed)**
3. **Close signal:** Unknown. **Signal: No (assumed)**

**Score: 0/3 under strict Mode C defaults.**

However — the MMF signals here are unknown, not negative. The difference matters. A "No" from absence of evidence is not the same as a confirmed product-market failure. The brief is too terse to distinguish "we have zero customers" from "we have 20 customers but didn't mention them."

**Verdict: Borderline — treated as 2/3 for campaign planning purposes, with a mandatory week-0 validation check.**

The campaign plan proceeds conditionally. Before executing Stages 4–5 at full scale, the user must complete one validation check in week 0:

- **If you have ≥10 paying or trialing users:** Extract three verbatim sentences from those users describing why they needed your tool. This is your language signal. You pass 3/3 MMF and should run the full plan.
- **If you have fewer than 10 paying or trialing users:** Run a 2-week validation sprint (5 structured discovery calls with target family lawyers, a free-trial offer to 20 cold outreach targets, track who converts) before committing to the full content and SEO build. Do not invest in an SEO flywheel for an offer nobody has confirmed they want.

The full plan below is written assuming the user passes the validation check. If the user discovers they are at 0/3 MMF after the week-0 sprint, the plan should be paused and the validation cycle repeated.

---

## 4. Channel Tier Stack

**Primary function: Demand capture** — family lawyers searching for affordable, niche-specific research tools represent existing intent. The tool needs to be findable before it needs to be persuasive.

**Sector rider: b2b-saas** — biases toward founder-LinkedIn content + SEO long-tail demand capture. Referral flywheel activates once ≥20 satisfied users exist. Community-build is secondary until the user base is large enough to seed it.

**Asymmetry constraint: Categorical (1:100+)** — Tier 3 and Tier 4 channels are blocked. No cold paid. No broad retargeting until organic winners exist and budget is available.

| Priority | Channel | Tier | Weekly Effort | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Long-tail SEO (family-law research queries) | Tier 2 | 2.5h | One article/week targeting specific family-law research queries. Compound effect; slow start, durable returns. |
| 2 | Founder LinkedIn content | Tier 1 | 1.5h | Two posts/week: one counter-narrative pricing post, one practitioner tip or case study. No stock images. |
| 3 | Bar association community presence (forums, Facebook groups, Slack) | Tier 1 | 1h | Provide genuine help in 2–3 communities. No pitching. Build authority before mentioning the tool. |
| 4 | Direct outreach to trial candidates (DMs, cold email) | Tier 1 | 0.5h | Target solo family lawyers identified via LinkedIn, Avvo, or local bar directories. 5 outreach attempts/week. |
| 5 | Tool directory / review site presence | Tier 2 | 0.5h (setup only) | Capterra, G2, Product Hunt (legal-tech category). One-time setup + monthly review-response. |

**Total: 6h/week — exactly at stated capacity ceiling. No cuts required.**

Paid channels (Tier 3/4): blocked at categorical asymmetry. Revisit only after organic traction is established and budget grows.

---

## 5. 70/30 (or 80/20) Allocation

**Allocation: 100% organic / 0% paid.**

At 100x asymmetry, the 70/30 framework does not apply in the conventional sense. The 30% that would normally go to paid amplification is redirected to organic channels with the highest compounding potential (SEO and community authority).

If the tool reaches 50+ paying users and monthly recurring revenue supports a small paid budget, the first paid dollar should go to:
- **Retargeting only** — warm audiences who visited the site but did not convert (not cold audiences).
- **After a 24–48h organic traction gate** — no post gets boosted until it has demonstrated genuine organic signal (saves, shares, substantive comments — not raw likes).
- **Never bid on your own brand-keyword** without a geo-holdout lift test first (see Section 10).

---

## 6. Competitor Saturation Map

### LexisNexis

**(a) What they saturate:**
LexisNexis saturates the enterprise register across every channel: authoritative white papers, polished case studies, conference sponsorships at major bar association events, and search-ad dominance on high-volume legal-research queries. Their content assumes a law firm with a dedicated research librarian. Their visual style is institutional — dark blues, formal typography, credential-heavy. Their emotional register is authority, comprehensiveness, and risk mitigation ("never miss a case"). Their CTAs are enterprise sales demos, not self-serve trials.

**(b) The absence that becomes your signal:**
LexisNexis cannot speak to a solo family lawyer at 11pm who needs to know if a specific custody precedent applies in their jurisdiction before tomorrow's hearing — not without routing them through an enterprise onboarding flow that presupposes a billing department. Their comprehensiveness is their blindspot: they optimize for "nothing missed" at a firm level, not "this specific answer, fast, for this specific solo practitioner." Your refusal to be comprehensive is the positioning: you are the tool built for family law, not for every law.

**The other major legal-research platforms** saturate similar enterprise and mid-market channels, reinforcing the same comprehensiveness-and-authority register. The absence of niche specificity is an industry-wide gap, not just a LexisNexis gap.

**(c) One-sentence positioning line:**
"The only research tool built for family lawyers, not for everyone who happens to do family law sometimes."

This line must appear in every piece of content, every outreach message, every landing page. It is the single sentence the campaign reinforces.

---

## 7. Three Alternative Campaign Shapes

### Shape 1 — Search-Capture-First

**Execution:** Build the SEO flywheel before anything else. Publish one deeply specific family-law research article per week (e.g., "How to research QDRO enforcement precedents in [State] without a full-suite subscription," "Family law case research checklist for custody hearings"). After 90 days, you own long-tail queries that LexisNexis's broad SEO strategy ignores. Acquisition is pull — lawyers find you when they need you.

**Tradeoffs:**
- Pros: Durable, compounding, zero paid dependency, builds domain authority.
- Cons: Slow start (60–90 days before meaningful traffic). Requires genuine practitioner knowledge to write credible content. No social virality inherent.
- Fits if: Founder can write or has access to a practicing family lawyer to co-create content.

**Flagship content (Self / Us / Now):**
- *Self:* "I built this tool because I watched [specific practitioner type] pay $400/month for access to 90% of a database they used 10% of."
- *Us:* "Family lawyers handling custody and divorce are systematically overcharged for legal research designed for BigLaw — we're the tool that doesn't make you pay for what you don't use."
- *Now:* "We're offering 30-day free access to the first 20 solo family lawyers who want to try it — no credit card, no enterprise demo call."

---

### Shape 2 — Community-First

**Execution:** Before any public content or SEO investment, seed a private community (Slack or Circle) of 10–20 solo family lawyers. Founder posts daily for 14 consecutive days — practice tips, case research shortcuts, jurisdiction-specific tools. The community earns trust before the tool is pitched. After 30 days, the community members become the distribution network: they post, recommend, and invite colleagues.

**Tradeoffs:**
- Pros: Highest trust compounding per hour invested. Generates authentic testimonials and language signals. Community members become evangelists.
- Cons: Requires daily founder commitment for ≥14 days (non-negotiable; dead communities are worse than no community). Slow to scale. Works only if the founder has genuine practitioner authority or a co-founder who does.
- Fits if: Founder is willing and able to post daily for 30 days, and has at least 10 personal contacts in the family-law space to seed the community.

**Week 0 requirement (before any public announcement):** Choose platform, write community rules, send 10 personal DMs to family lawyers in your network. Do not launch publicly until the room has activity.

**Flagship content (Self / Us / Now):**
- *Self:* Same as Shape 1.
- *Us:* "This is a community for solo and small-firm family lawyers who are tired of paying enterprise rates for tools that assume you have a research department."
- *Now:* "We're keeping it to 50 members for now — if you're doing family law and want to test tools that actually fit your practice, reply here."

---

### Shape 3 — Earned-Media-First

**Execution:** Identify 3–5 legal-tech journalists and bar association publications that cover legal-tech for small firms and solo practitioners. Pitch a specific, newsworthy story: "Solo family lawyers are paying LexisNexis enterprise rates while using 10% of the platform — here's how we built a tool that costs less and does what they actually need." The story works because it has a protagonist (solo family lawyers), an antagonist (enterprise pricing mismatch), and a resolution (your tool). One press mention in a credible legal-tech outlet drives more qualified trial signups than months of cold outreach.

**Tradeoffs:**
- Pros: High trust transfer from editorial coverage. No content production at scale required. One placement can drive meaningful inbound.
- Cons: Zero control over timing or outcome. Requires a genuinely newsworthy angle (the tool must exist, work, and have at least some real users to be credible). Research is week-1 work — specific publication targets must be identified before outreach (see Section 8).
- Fits if: The founder has a credible story and the tool has at least a few real users who can be quoted.

**Flagship content (Self / Us / Now):**
- Same Self/Us/Now framework as above, formatted as a pitch email rather than a post.

---

## 8. First-30-Days Action List

**Capacity ceiling: 6h/week. Primary shape: Search-Capture-First (Shape 1) with community seeding from Shape 2 as a parallel track in weeks 2–4.**

### Week 0 (before publishing anything)

| Action | Effort | Owner | Success Signal |
|---|---|---|---|
| MMF validation: list your current paying/trialing users; extract 3 verbatim sentences from them about why they needed your tool | 1h | Founder | You have the language signal before writing any content |
| Identify 3 family-law bar association communities (Facebook groups, Slack groups, forums) where solo practitioners congregate | 0.5h | Founder | List of 3 active communities with >100 members |
| Identify 20 keyword targets: family-law research queries with low competition (use Google Search Console, Ahrefs free tier, or AnswerThePublic) | 0.5h | Founder | A spreadsheet of 20 queries, ranked by estimated volume and competition |
| Earned-media target research: identify 5 legal-tech publications or newsletters that cover tools for solo/small-firm practitioners | 1h | Founder | A list of 5 targets with editor name, contact channel, and one recent article to reference in pitch |

*Week 0 total: 3h (one-time setup investment, does not count against recurring 6h/week ceiling)*

---

### Week 1

| Action | Effort | Owner | Success Signal |
|---|---|---|---|
| Publish first SEO article: pick the highest-volume, lowest-competition query from your keyword list; write 1,200–1,800 words of practitioner-specific content | 2.5h | Founder | Article published; submitted to Google Search Console for indexing |
| Post counter-narrative pricing post on LinkedIn (Concept A): show the real cost breakdown of a LexisNexis seat vs. your tool for a solo family lawyer doing 10 research sessions/month | 1h | Founder | Post published; track saves, shares, substantive comments over 48h |
| Join 2 bar association communities; introduce yourself with a genuinely helpful answer to an existing question (no pitch) | 0.5h | Founder | Member in 2 communities; one substantive reply posted |
| Send DMs to 5 solo family lawyers on LinkedIn or via bar directory; offer 30-day free trial in exchange for a 15-minute feedback call after 30 days | 0.5h | Founder | 5 messages sent; track reply rate |
| Set up tool profile on Capterra and G2 (legal-tech category) | 0.5h | Founder | Profile live with accurate description and one screenshot |

*Week 1 total: 5.5h*

---

### Week 2

| Action | Effort | Owner | Success Signal |
|---|---|---|---|
| Publish second SEO article: next keyword from list | 2.5h | Founder | Article published; internal link from article 1 |
| Post one LinkedIn practitioner tip (not a pitch — a genuinely useful family-law research shortcut or jurisdiction tip) | 0.5h | Founder | Post published; observe organic reach |
| Community presence: answer 2 questions in bar association communities with substantive help | 0.5h | Founder | Two replies posted; watch for DMs or profile visits |

*Week 2 total: 5h*

---

### Week 3

| Action | Effort | Owner | Success Signal |
|---|---|---|---|
| Publish third SEO article | 2.5h | Founder | Article published; check Search Console for early impressions on articles 1–2 |
| LinkedIn post: share one real user story (with permission) — what they were trying to research, how long it took, what it cost | 1h | Founder | Post published; watch for saves and shares as proof of resonance |

| Send DMs to 5 new trial candidates | 0.5h | Founder | 5 messages sent |

*Week 3 total: 5h*

---

### Week 4

| Action | Effort | Owner | Success Signal |
|---|---|---|---|
| Publish fourth SEO article | 2.5h | Founder | Article published |
| LinkedIn post: pricing counter-narrative follow-up — "30 days in, here's what solo family lawyers told us about their research costs" | 1h | Founder | Post published; substantive comment thread is the success signal |
| Community presence: answer 2 questions | 0.5h | Founder | Two replies |

**Cuts from draft:** A community node (Slack/Circle) was initially drafted for weeks 2–4 but was cut because it would add 3–4h/week of daily posting commitment and exceed the 6h/week ceiling. Re-add in month 2 if a second team member can manage daily community posts, or if the founder's capacity increases.

---

## 9. Ad Copy + Boost Rules

**Categorical asymmetry — broad cold-paid creative is refused.** At 100x spend asymmetry, cold audience paid creative will not generate statistically distinguishable lift from zero. Every dollar spent on cold paid is a dollar not invested in the SEO flywheel that compounds.

**Retargeting only (future state):** Once the tool has ≥50 monthly site visitors and ≥10 paying users, consider retargeting-only campaigns targeting:
- Site visitors who visited the pricing page but did not convert.
- Warm lookalikes based on your first 20 paying users.

**Boost rule (if any post is considered for paid amplification):**
- The 24–48h organic traction gate is mandatory. A post must demonstrate genuine organic signal (saves, shares, substantive comments — not raw likes) before any boost decision.
- Boost budget cap: no more than $50/post until organic ROI is measured via Template 5 (Section 10).
- Frequency cap: no audience sees the same post more than 3 times.

**Sample organic post direction (no budget needed):**

Format: Plain text LinkedIn post. No stock images. No designed carousel. No agency language.

> "Quick breakdown: what a solo family lawyer actually pays for LexisNexis vs. what they actually use.
>
> Average seat cost: [public pricing tier].
> Average family-law research sessions per month for a solo practitioner: [your data or publicly cited estimate].
> Cost per session: [math].
>
> We built [Tool Name] for the practitioner who doesn't need 40 million documents — they need the right 400, fast, for the practice area they actually do.
>
> 30-day free trial. No enterprise demo call. [Link]"

The specific numbers must be real — do not publish without verifying. If you do not have LexisNexis pricing data, use their publicly listed rates or quote a practitioner who shared their invoice.

---

## 10. Lift-Test / Measurement Plan

**Template 5 — Organic Source Attribution** (zero-budget template; mandatory at this asymmetry level)

**Hypothesis:** Among organic channels (LinkedIn founder content, SEO articles, bar association community presence, direct outreach), channel X will outperform channel Y on conversion rate per unique visitor to the trial signup page.

**Setup:**
- Tag every inbound link with UTM parameters: `utm_source=linkedin`, `utm_source=seo`, `utm_source=community`, `utm_source=outreach`, `utm_source=directory`.
- Track in Google Analytics 4 (free) or your existing analytics stack.
- Measure: unique visitors per channel, trial signups per channel, conversion rate per channel.

**Duration:** 30 days (weeks 1–4 of the plan above).

**Decision threshold:**
- Top channel (highest conversion rate): double effort allocation in month 2.
- Bottom 20% of channels by conversion rate: cut or reduce to maintenance level. Reinvest the saved time in the top channel.
- Explicit caveat: Template 5 is directional only. It measures attributed conversion rate, not incrementality. It will overcount channels that touch users late in the journey and undercount channels that generate awareness early. Use it to cut obvious losers, not to declare obvious winners.

**What success looks like in 30 days:**
- ≥3 SEO articles indexed and generating impressions (even if not converting yet — indexing is the 30-day success signal for SEO).
- ≥5 trial signups from non-direct channels.
- At least one channel showing a conversion rate materially higher than the others.
- At least one earned-media pitch reply (not necessarily a placement — a reply is a signal).

---

## 11. Anti-Vanity Metric Dashboard

### Track (these are real signals)

| Metric | Why it matters |
|---|---|
| Trial signups (by UTM source) | Tells you which channel is actually converting |
| Paying users (week-over-week) | The only revenue signal that matters in month 1 |
| SEO article impressions (Search Console) | Tells you whether you're building domain authority; expect slow growth for 60–90 days |
| LinkedIn post saves and shares | Shares = resonance; saves = intent to return. Likes lie. |
| Community DMs and inbound outreach | Tells you word-of-mouth is activating |
| Trial-to-paid conversion rate | Tells you whether the product (not the campaign) is the bottleneck |
| Earned-media pitch reply rate | A reply rate below 10% means the pitch angle needs revision |
| Verbatim user language (extraction) | The single most valuable input for future content — what exact words do users use to describe their pain? |

### Ignore (these will mislead you)

| Metric | Why it misleads |
|---|---|
| Impressions / reach / CPM | Reach above the first 5–6 impressions does not persuade — it annoys. Impressions are not conversions. |
| LinkedIn follower count | A vanity metric. 200 followers who are solo family lawyers beat 2,000 followers who are marketers watching you for content inspiration. |
| Raw likes | Cheap, fast, and tell you almost nothing about intent. |
| Platform-reported ROAS (if you ever run paid) | Platforms are graded on attributed conversions; you are graded on actual lift. These are not the same thing. Require an incremental lift test before trusting any platform ROAS figure. |
| Website traffic (without conversion breakdown) | Traffic without UTM segmentation is noise. 100 visitors from a relevant bar association community beat 1,000 visitors from a generic legal directory. |
| Time-on-site / bounce rate | Family lawyers are busy. A lawyer who reads your pricing page for 45 seconds and signs up is more valuable than a lawyer who reads 3 blog posts and leaves. |

---

*Confirm or adjust any row in the Assumptions table and I will re-run the affected stages.*
